---
title: "Validation"
---

```{r}
coelho_summary_path <- "data/coelho-basis-benchmark-summary.csv"
coelho_bench <- if (file.exists(coelho_summary_path)) {
  read.csv(coelho_summary_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
} else {
  NULL
}
```

## Coelho-first validation

The validation story on the website is centered on the bundled Coelho spectra.
That keeps the example aligned with the research note while still checking the
core matrix factorization behavior of `spectral_unmix()`.

## Coelho basis benchmark

The figure below uses the bundled Coelho demo spectra as the true basis, then
generates a synthetic mixture matrix from them. That gives us a controlled
comparison in which the target basis is known and both `SpectralUnmix` and
`NMF::nmf()` can be compared on the same problem.

```{r}
if (file.exists("images/coelho-basis-benchmark.png")) {
  knitr::include_graphics("images/coelho-basis-benchmark.png")
} else {
  cat("Coelho benchmark figure not found in `site/images/coelho-basis-benchmark.png`.\n")
}
```

```{r}
if (!is.null(coelho_bench)) {
  coelho_bench$reconstruction_mse <- signif(coelho_bench$reconstruction_mse, 4)
  coelho_bench$basis_mse <- signif(coelho_bench$basis_mse, 4)
  knitr::kable(coelho_bench, caption = "Coelho basis benchmark summary.")
} else {
  cat("Coelho benchmark summary CSV not found.\n")
}
```

For this benchmark, the meaningful comparison is after component matching and
normalization. The curves should lie close to one another, but exact equality
is not expected because:

- NMF solutions are only identifiable up to permutation and scaling.
- `SpectralUnmix` and `NMF::nmf()` use different optimizers.
- once smoothness regularization is turned on, the two objectives are no longer
  the same.

## What is checked

The package test suite currently verifies three things:

- `spectral_unmix()` recovers a noise-free low-rank factorization with small
  reconstruction and component-spectrum error.
- `spectral_unmix()` is competitive with `NMF::nmf(method = "lee")` on the
  same toy problem after matching fitted components to the reference ordering.
- early stopping and convergence bookkeeping are exposed through `loss`,
  `niter_run`, and `converged`.

These checks live in:

- `tests/testthat/test-vanilla-nmf-reference.R`

## Why compare to `NMF`

The `NMF` package is a natural baseline for the unregularized matrix
factorization problem. Matching that reference behavior on a simple benchmark
gives us confidence that the torch-based optimizer in `SpectralUnmix` is
recovering the expected NMF structure before smoothness regularization or IFU
data handling are layered on top.

## Running the validation locally

```r
devtools::test(filter = "vanilla-nmf")
```

The benchmark tests require both `torch` and `NMF`. If either package is not
available in the local R environment, the corresponding tests are skipped.

## Interpreting the diagnostics

- `fit$loss` is the optimization history.
- `fit$niter_run` is the number of iterations actually executed.
- `fit$converged` indicates whether early stopping triggered before the maximum
  iteration budget.

For practical work, the benchmark is intended as a sanity check. Good behavior
here means the core factorization engine is behaving as expected; difficult
real-data fits can then be investigated as modeling or preprocessing issues
rather than as a basic failure of the package optimizer.
